label: 01 · For off-lead fell walking
name: Long Line / Training Lead
description: If you're nervous about letting your dog off lead, a 10–20m long line lets them explore freely on open fells while keeping control near sheep, cliff edges and other walkers. Pack a spare short lead too.

label: 02 · Lake District specific risk
name: Tick Remover
description: Bracken, heather and long grass are all tick territory. Check your dog after every walk — ears, armpits, groin, between the toes. A proper tick remover tool (not fingers) is the only safe way to remove them. Treat with a flea and tick preventative before you travel.

label: 03 · Rocky terrain. Cuts happen
name: Dog First Aid Kit
description: Even more important here than in the city. A basic kit: antiseptic wipes, sterile gauze pads, self-adhesive bandage (Vetrap) and tweezers for thorn removal. There are no 24-hour emergency vets inside the national park — know your nearest options before you travel.

label: 04 · No signal. Don't rely on your phone
name: GPS Dog Tracker
description: Signal is patchy or non-existent across most of the park. Even if your dog is used to being off lead, they can get lost in a new area. A GPS tracker on their collar is the safety net your phone can't be — especially ones that work independently of phone signal.

label: 05 · Dog-specific travel essentials
name: Dog Car Kit
description: A properly fitted harness or secured crate — not loose in the boot. Boot liner and at least two spare towels (they will be used). A travel water bowl and bottle for summit stops. Dog-safe antiseptic spray for paw clean-ups on the go.
